title: New Zealand Migration Tracker
text:
Net NZ citizen migration peaked at +24,572 in the 12 months to October 2020 while the border was closed and departures collapsed. By March 2026 it stood at -36,538. Over the same 12 months, non-NZ citizens (including Australians) recorded a net +60,787 — down from the post-reopening peak of +176,489 in October 2023.
                                                


Source: Statistics NZ ITM552301

title: New Zealand Migration Tracker
text:

title: New Zealand Migration Tracker
section: The post-COVID surge was overwhelmingly young
text:
The post-COVID surge was overwhelmingly young
Net migration is dominated by people in their 20s and 30s throughout the series. The post-2022 cycle saw those cohorts reach levels far above anything in the pre-COVID baseline before the recent slowdown.
                                                


Source: Statistics NZ ITM552101

title: New Zealand Migration Tracker
section: India now supplies one in four non-NZ arrivals
text:
India now supplies one in four non-NZ arrivals
The composition of net migration has shifted markedly since 2022. India and China now dominate inflows, while the “Other” category has compressed as migration becomes more concentrated.
                                                


Source: Statistics NZ ITM552301

The share chart shows each country’s arrivals as a proportion of all non-NZ arrivals on a 12-month rolling average.
                                                


Source: Statistics NZ ITM552301

title: New Zealand Migration Tracker
section: Work visas, not students, powered the surge
text:
Work visas, not students, powered the surge
The arrivals chart shows the surge in visa mix terms — what proportion of people crossing the border held each visa type.
                                                


Source: Statistics NZ ITM552201

Approved work visa decisions peaked sharply in late 2022 as the AEWV replaced Essential Skills. Skilled Work approvals have since moderated following the April 2024 reforms, while Working Holiday and Relationship visas have recovered from their COVID-era lows.
                                                


Source: MBIE Migration Data Explorer

                                                


Source: Statistics NZ ITM553001

                                                


Source: Statistics NZ ITM553001

title: New Zealand Migration Tracker
section: Lower-skill work visa share reaches 49% for some nationalities
text:
Lower-skill work visa share reaches 49% for some nationalities
India’s work visa intake has shifted from high-skill toward lower-skill roles since the AEWV launched in mid-2022.
                                                


Source: MBIE Migration Data Explorer


Note: W3 covers records where INZ captured an occupation (~60% of approved work visa decisions). Open work right holders (Working Holiday, Relationship) are largely excluded. Roles with no recorded skill level are excluded from proportions.

India’s lower-skill share (36%) is above countries like the UK (16%) and China (11%) — but Vietnam (49%) and Fiji (48%) rank higher. The pattern is not India-specific.
                                                


Source: MBIE Migration Data Explorer


Note: W3 covers records where INZ captured an occupation (~60% of approved work visa decisions). Open work right holders (Working Holiday, Relationship) are largely excluded. Roles with no recorded skill level are excluded from proportions.

title: New Zealand Migration Tracker
section: Auckland absorbs most arrivals; Queenstown punches above its weight
text:
Auckland absorbs most arrivals; Queenstown punches above its weight
International migration is concentrated in a handful of Territorial Authorities. Auckland and Hamilton lead by absolute numbers, but smaller TAs like Queenstown-Lakes rank higher on a per-capita basis. The map shows cumulative net international migration per 1,000 residents over the three years ended June 2025 — teal indicates net inflow, red indicates net outflow.
                                                


Source: Statistics NZ, Subnational population estimates at 30 June 2025


Note: Population estimates are provisional.

The top 5 TAs by per-capita inflow, and a closer look at the 21 Auckland local board areas.
                                                


Source: Statistics NZ, Subnational population estimates at 30 June 2025


Note: Population estimates are provisional.
